coredata.dc:title: A highly efficient and recyclable living biocatalyst using Shewanella@polydopamine@NH2-doped carbon dot biohybrids and polypyrrole immobilized melamine foam for microbial-photoreduction of Cr(VI)

coredata.dc:description: A novel living biocomposite was engineered to boost environmental Cr(VI) remediation. Photosensitive biohybrids were firstly configured through an inward-to-outward assembly of NH2-doped carbon dots (NCDs), polydopamine (PDA) and wild Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 cells to generate microbial-photoreduction of Cr(VI) at the nanoscale. Then, biohybrid-derived biofilms were implanted onto the surface of melamine foam (MF) through an in-situ polypyrrole (PPy)-heterojunction to form highly conductive living MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites at a macroscale. Bacterial biomass immobilized onto the surface of MF and the efficacy of related biocomposites for Cr(VI) reduction increased with increasing pyrrole (PY) monomer concentrations (0.15 → 0.60 mL). In a 50 mg/L-Cr(VI) reduction system conducted under visible light illumination, biocomposites treated with 0.60 mL PY monomer displayed the highest Cr(VI) reduction efficiency (100%) with the shortest reaction time (24 h) during the first reduction cycle and significantly outperformed other biocomposite formulations over longer time periods (36–72 h). The living biocomposites exhibited an outstanding recyclability (>4 cycles) in subsequent reduction cycles. Overall, a reliable amalgamation of all living/non-living components integrated into the biocomposite ensured an efficient biocatalyst framework for Cr(VI) reduction and recyclable use.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Cr(VI) removal by treatment with different hybrid cells was described using a pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model. Further, Cr(VI) reduction efficiency (corresponding to average Cr(VI) removal degree by individual cells and reaction time), as well as the overall TOC consumption and related TOC consumption per cell, were calculated according to Equations S1–S3 in supporting information. A more detailed discussion for estimation of Cr(VI) removal and TOC consumption is reported in Method S3 of SM. Data were analyzed and displayed using Origin 9.0, Microsoft Excel 2016 and SPSS 20.0 software. Each data point represents the average value of at least three measurements. Standard deviations for triplicate analyses were reported as error bars in the corresponding graphs. Treatment differences were examined by analysis of variance using SPSS with a 95% confidence level.Decorated biocomposites using greater PY monomer amounts (0.45 and 0.60 mL) for junction formation achieve a considerably stronger performance (Fig. 8c–d). In particular, the biocomposites modified with 0.60 mL of PY monomer display the highest efficiency and shortest reaction times (only 24 h) for complete Cr(VI) reduction during the first reduction cycle; efficiency remained high, but reaction time was extended to 60 h by the fourth reaction cycle (Fig. 8d and Table S2). Since MF-derived composites contain a porous structure and PPy-coated layers are positively charged, the negatively charged Cr(VI) ions are readily adsorbed to the MF skeleton through electrostatic attraction (L. Zhang et al., 2020). This Cr adsorption effect leads to an attenuated biocatalytic effect in subsequent reduction cycles due to a lower effective contact area among PPy-junction sites resulting from deposition of Cr(III)-bearing precipitates. This mechanism is supported by the Cr(T) adsorption dynamics across reduction cycles and the EDS distribution (Fig. 4h). Namely, there is a remarkable attenuation of Cr(T) adsorption performance in subsequent reduction cycles in the presence of biocomposites as Cr(III)-bearing precipitates accumulate (Fig. S9). Moreover, no black precipitates derived from PPy were leached before or after a reaction cycle (Fig. S10) indicating a stable PPy-junction in the biocomposites that inhibited leaching throughout progressive reaction cycles.This work was supported by the Zhejiang Province Public Welfare Technology Application Research Project (LGF22E080002), Start-up fund to Xiu-Feng Huang from the Second Affiliated Hospital and Yuying Children's Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University and Wenzhou Municipal Science and Technology Bureau of China (N2023011).

This work was supported by the Zhejiang Province Public Welfare Technology Application Research Project ( LGF22E080002 ), Start-up fund to Xiu-Feng Huang from the Second Affiliated Hospital and Yuying Children's Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University and Wenzhou Municipal Science and Technology Bureau of China ( N2023011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the stepwise fabrication of living MF-derived biocomposites including the preparation of living biohybrids, PPy-polymerization and bacterial adhesion.

A comparison of color differences for bacterial fluids under purple light irradiation and TEM images of the morphology of wild strain and fabricated biohybrids. TEM images from left-to-right indicate the cellular morphologies and related deposits on cell surfaces under higher magnification. Yellow and red arrows indicate the presence of PDA layer and NCDs, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Removal dynamics of Cr(VI) (a), pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting of Cr(VI) removal (b), OD600 value change (c), maximal Cr(VI) reduction efficiency (d), overall consumption of TOC by active wild strain and biohybrid cells (e), and relative quantification of TOC per cell at 120 h (f) under dark or visible light illumination (mean ± std dev; n = 3). (Note: WS = wild strain, [OD600]t and [OD600]o refer to OD600 value at time t and o).

Representative images (a), relating size (b) of MF and MF/PPy composites and the compressibility of MF/PPy composites via manual pinch test (c). Morphology of MF/PPy composite skeletons decorated with gradient amounts of PPy by SEM characterization (d). MF composites decorated with 0.15, 0.30, 0.45 and 0.60 mL of PY monomer are denoted as MF/PPy-1, MF/PPy-2, MF/PPy-3 and MF/PPy-4, respectively. SEM images (e) of a slice cut from the MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposite decorated with 0.60 mL PY monomer after 4 reduction cycles and related overlapping (f), EDS maps (g) and distribution (h) of C, N, Cr and Fe within the slice. Morphologies of biofilms formed by wild strain versus biohybrids/PPy by SEM observation (i and j).

FT-IR spectra (a), specific surface area and pore volume (mean ± std dev) (b), and electrical conductivity (mean ± std dev) (c) of different MF/PPy composites. MF composites decorated with 0.15, 0.30, 0.45 and 0.60 mL of PY monomer are denoted as MF/PPy-1, MF/PPy-2, MF/PPy-3 and MF/PPy-4, respectively. XPS Cr 2p spectrum (d) for precipitates collected from MF/PPy/biohybrid-4 composites.

Removal dynamics of Cr(VI) in the presence of different active/inactive composites adhered to biofilms comprising wild strain (a and b) and biohybrids (c and d) with/without PPy-heterojunctions under dark or visible light illumination (mean ± std dev; n = 3).
Comparison of Cr(VI) reduction efficiency and reaction times in the presence of different active biocomposites under dark or visible light illumination (mean ± std dev; n = 3).

Removal dynamics of Cr(VI) in the presence of different living MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites decorated with 0.15 (a), 0.30 (b), 0.45 (c) and 0.60 (d) mL PY monomer throughout four reduction cycles under visible light illumination (mean ± std dev; n = 3).

Evaluation of the stability of immobilized residual biomass (a) and dispersive biomass (b) measured by BCA assay and OD600 values, respectively, in the presence of different living MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites throughout four reduction cycles under visible light illumination (mean ± std dev; n = 3; mean values with same lower case letters are not significantly different at P = 0.05).
Schematic of the architecture for the dynamic living MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites, which encompasses the functionalities and feature complexities across spatial scales.
Comparison of recent investigations examining Cr(VI) reduction by the treatment with Shewanella genus and specific mediating material.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: According to the OD600 versus biomass value relationship (Fig. S6), biomass with an OD600 of 0.3 in this work corresponds to ∼0.38 g/L.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel living biocomposite was engineered to boost environmental Cr(VI) remediation. Photosensitive biohybrids were firstly configured through an inward-to-outward assembly of NH2-doped carbon dots (NCDs), polydopamine (PDA) and wild Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 cells to generate microbial-photoreduction of Cr(VI) at the nanoscale. Then, biohybrid-derived biofilms were implanted onto the surface of melamine foam (MF) through an in-situ polypyrrole (PPy)-heterojunction to form highly conductive living MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites at a macroscale. Bacterial biomass immobilized onto the surface of MF and the efficacy of related biocomposites for Cr(VI) reduction increased with increasing pyrrole (PY) monomer concentrations (0.15 → 0.60 mL). In a 50 mg/L-Cr(VI) reduction system conducted under visible light illumination, biocomposites treated with 0.60 mL PY monomer displayed the highest Cr(VI) reduction efficiency (100%) with the shortest reaction time (24 h) during the first reduction cycle and significantly outperformed other biocomposite formulations over longer time periods (36–72 h). The living biocomposites exhibited an outstanding recyclability (>4 cycles) in subsequent reduction cycles. Overall, a reliable amalgamation of all living/non-living components integrated into the biocomposite ensured an efficient biocatalyst framework for Cr(VI) reduction and recyclable use.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)] is a pollutant of global concern to human health that mainly originates from electroplating wastewater and the leather industry (Y. Jiang et al., 2022). Among various pollutants, Cr(VI) has a distinct toxicity in human health as well as impacting soil/sediment microbial communities (A. Monga et al., 2022), which pose a threat to aquatic life and humans through food and drinking water ingestion. Considering Cr(VI) is mobile, highly carcinogenic and difficult to remove by direct precipitation (Y. Li et al., 2019), effective new strategies for Cr(VI) remediation are urgently required. Cr(VI) remediation is generally achieved through specific adsorption and reduction treatments (Y. Zhang et al., 2017). Among a variety of treatments, microbial Cr(VI) bioremediation has several advantages including economic, environmentally friendly and technological feasibility attributes (Y. Jiang et al., 2022; Y. Li et al., 2019). The bioremediation process involves microbial reduction of highly toxic Cr(VI)-bearing anions to insoluble and less toxic Cr(III)-bearing precipitates by specialized electrically active bacteria (EAB) (J. F. Gutiérrez-Corona et al., 2016). Due to the fragile biological tolerance and limited efficacy of natural EABs (C. C. Zhao et al., 2022), conventional microbial remediation strategies mediated by extracellular electron transfer (EET) processes are generally inefficient under real-world environmental conditions (Y. Li et al., 2020). Notably, the merits of photosensitive biohybrids used for Cr(VI) reduction that incorporate microbial reduction with photoreduction greatly surpass the inherent bottleneck incurred by natural microbes (A. Chawla et al., 2023), thereby increasing the efficacy of microbial Cr(VI) bioremediation.

Concerning the production of a bulk electron flux, materials are often designed to use native cellular machineries as a chassis for living photosensitive biohybrids. These semiconductor nanoparticle (NP)-derived biohybrids produce bio-electrons and harness specific semiconductor NPs as accessories to harvest additional foreign photoelectrons upon effective irradiation (G. Dong et al., 2020a; G. Dong et al., 2020b). Hence, engineering of photosensitive biohybrids represents a tailored optimization for natural cells to enable a higher efficiency of charge transfer than from dispersed individual cells. Because NH2-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are positively charged, intrinsically reductive and readily responsive to visible-light irradiation (N. Dhenadhayalan et al., 2020; D. Guo et al., 2019), biohybrids that are coated with NCDs are more successful than most traditional metal-based semiconductor NP-derived biohybrids (e.g., biohybrids assembled with CdS, TiO2 and InP semiconductor NPs) (Z. Chen et al., 2022; A. Hu et al., 2022; J. Ye et al., 2022).

For Cr(VI) reduction mediated by conventional EAB-derived EET processes, Cr(VI) is utilized as a terminal electron acceptor to harvest bio-electrons produced from microbial degradation of labile organic carbon substrates to form Cr(III) (Y. Li et al., 2019). Given that more photoelectrons are produced from the photoreduction pathway, the incorporation of irradiated semiconductor NPs gives a large boost to Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of photosensitive biohybrids (J. Li et al., 2022). Although the EET performance of single cells can be effectively improved through engineering photosensitive biohybrids, Cr(VI) reduction relying on dispersed individual cells is usually less efficient and time-consuming (K. Fang et al., 2020). This highlights the potential for large-scale incorporation of biofilms to enhance the joint efforts of individual cells to maximize EET efficiency (R. Renslow et al., 2013; M. D. Yates et al., 2017). Notably, biofilms developed under natural conditions contain little biomass, resulting in a sparse spatial distribution pattern and poor conductivity (M. Matsukawa and E. P. Greenberg, 2004). Thus, improving the stability of biofilms is a key component of biofilm-related research. Therein, engineering hybrid conductive material/biofilm biocomposites by grafting biofilms with biocompatible and high electrically conductive NPs is a successful strategy to ensure the formation of aggregated biofilms that favor surface/interface dynamic reactions (W. Wang et al., 2016; X. Xie et al., 2015; C. Yang et al., 2020). Polypyrrole (PPy) has been widely used as a promising biocompatible conductive nano-polymer for in-situ formation of highly conductive heterojunctions due to its reversible doping/de-doping properties, high conductivity and strong adhesion to substrates (Z. Chen et al., 2022; X. Qi et al., 2021; R.-B. Song et al., 2017). Successful formation of aggregated biofilms will greatly facilitate electron transfer across larger spatial and temporal ranges and decrease energy losses associated with EET processes.

To mitigate energy consumption and promote green chemistry technologies, efforts to develop reusable solid biocatalysts have gained particular attention for Cr(VI) bioremediation (Q. Luo et al., 2019). This implies that fabrication of a reusable solid biocatalyst requires a durable solid material with robust chemical and physical stability that provides abundant attachment sites for bacterial adhesion. As a flexible nano-sponge, melamine foam (MF) has a stable chemical structure and 3D cross-linking porous network (Y. Sun et al., 2019), which endows reusability and versatile functions for PPy in-situ polymerization and biofilm aggregation. The abundance of nitrogen atoms contained within the PPy rings leads to increased hydrophilicity (W. Qian et al., 2017), that in turn greatly increases the affinity of attached biofilms at bacteria-PPy junction interfaces (R.-B. Song et al., 2017). Hence, modular configurations utilizing MF as a skeleton and PPy for immobilizing bulk biofilms are a very promising design for developing biocatalysts for rapid, robust and recyclable remediation of Cr(VI).

Herein, a novel integrated and recyclable living biocatalyst was engineered and evaluated for Cr(VI) bioremediation of wastewater. The design of living biocatalysts comprises the architectural parameters related to which artificial scaffolds are selected as the main framework, and what kinds of building blocks are assembled to optimize charge transfer at a given scale, as well as the design approach. Photosensitive hybrid cells were firstly modified through harnessing a representative Cr(VI)-reducing strain (Shewanella oneidensis MR-1) with NCDs and PDA, which were then grafted to the MF skeleton to produce aggregated biofilms through PPy-junctions. The modulation process to maximize interfacial charge transport within living biomaterial was rigorously optimized from nanoscale (biohybrid cells) to microscale (biofilms) and finally to the macroscale (a bulk biocomposite). Moreover, we examined the capability of living biocatalysts to serve as a recyclable material for successive cycles in Cr(VI) bioremediation. The fabricated living biocomposite featuring high efficiency, energy-saving and recyclable attributes represents an advanced biomaterial with excellent potential for use in remediating Cr(VI) pollution.

Synthesis of NCDs was conducted according to previously reported protocols (S. Liu et al., 2021). In brief, 0.1 g of dried chopped rice straw is sequentially washed with Milli-Q water and ethanol (99.5%, purchased from J&K Scientific Co., China), and then mixed with 30 mL Milli-Q water in a 50 mL-Teflon reactor. The mixture is sealed in the Teflon reactor and heated to 180 °C for 5 h, followed by cooling to room temperature. Thereafter, the resulting mixture is harvested through centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitates, and then passed through a 0.22-μm nylon filter. Next, the mixture containing 10 mL filtered CD suspension and 536 μL ethylenediamine (≥99.5%, purchased from Macklin Co., China) are sealed in a 50 mL-Teflon reactor and heated at 180 °C for 5 h before finally cooling to room temperature to harvest the NCD stock solution. XRD characterization of the synthesized NCDs is shown in Fig. S1. As depicted from TEM and HRTEM images (Figs. S2a–b), the average size of NCDs is 1–3 nm. Characterization by UV–vis DRS and Kubelka-Munk treatment of the diffuse reflectance (M. A. Zanjanchi et al., 2006) determined the maximum absorption wavelength and intrinsic bandgap of the NCDs are 288 nm and 2.31 eV (Fig. S3), respectively. A photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of NCDs is shown in Fig. S4.

Wild strain S. oneidensis MR-1 was selected as a representative Cr(VI)-reducing model bacteria for this work. Wild strain was initially cultured on a Luria–Bertani (LB) agar plate, then propagated using aerobic LB medium at 30 °C in a shaker (200 rpm) for 12 h. A bacterial logistic growth curve is profiled based on the optical density at 600 nm (OD600) and corresponds to growth time (Fig. S5). Biohybrids are constructed through an inward-to-outward assembly of NCDs and polydopamine (PDA) to Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. In brief, wild strain is firstly aerobically incubated in NCD-containing LB medium to harvest the proliferated NCD-internalized cells. Thereafter, the enriched cells were configured with PDA shells in a Bis-Tris propane (BTP) matrix under an anaerobic atmosphere. Next, the resulting PDA-coated cells were decorated with NCDs to obtain a unique hybrid shell composed of PDA and NCDs under an anaerobic atmosphere in the dark. Finally, the resulting hybrid cells (denoted as biohybrids) were collected by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 3 min and washed twice with 2.0 mL BTP solution (10 mM). Additional details regarding biohybrid construction are provided in Method S1 of SM. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed (described in Method S2 of SM) to examine the electrochemical performance of wild strain and biohybrids. Nyquist plots of EIS for wild strain and biohybrid electrodes are shown in Fig. S6.

Pristine MF is equally cut into small pieces with a uniform cylinder size (10 mm Φ × 40 mm H) and then alternately washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol twice before drying to constant weight at 50 °C. Next, MF/PPy composites were prepared via an in-situ PPy-polymerization using pyrrole (PY) monomer as a modifier and FeCl3 as an oxidizing agent (H. J. Kharat et al., 2007; Y. Sun et al., 2019). To compare the effect of PPy-junctions on biofilm immobilization, specific MF/PPy composites decorated with gradient amounts of PPy were prepared. Briefly, 0.15, 0.30, 0.45 and 0.60 mL of PY monomers are in turn ultrasonically dissolved in 40 mL Milli-Q water for 5 min in a 100 mL conical flask. Firstly, MFs are fully soaked in a dispersive system and repeatedly pressed using a sterilized PTFE tweezers to ensure even infiltration into the foam; reaction systems were maintained at room temperature. Next, 40 mL FeCl3 solution (0.58 g FeCl3 per 0.15 mL PY monomer) was slowly injected into the above reaction system and magnetically stirred in an ice-bath for 4 h. Finally, the resulting composites (denoted as MF/PPy composites) were separated and repeatedly rinsed with Milli-Q water until the rinse solution became colorless, after which the composite was vacuum dried at 50 °C for 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of biofilm onto MF-derived composites
ce:section.ce:para: Different types of living MF-derived composites were prepared through grafting active or inactive bacterial cells (wild strain or biohybrids) to the skeletons of MF-derived (MF or MF/PPy) composites. For inactive MF-derived composites, an equal initial biomass of active bacterial cells was autoclaved at 120 °C for 20 min and then used for grafting on the skeletons of sterilized MF-derived composites. A 70 mL volume of wild strain or biohybrids enriched fluid (incubated in BTP medium with an initial OD600 value ∼1.0) was prepared in a 150-mL conical flask prior to use. Then, three pieces of dried sterilized MF-derived composites were fully soaked with bacterial fluids by repeatedly pressing the foam with a sterilized PTFE tweezers. Next, the soaked materials were incubated at 30 °C on a shaker at 150 rpm for 5 h. The MF-derived composites grafted with bacterial cells (denoted as living MF-derived biocomposites) were separated and stored in a sterilized Petri dish for subsequent use in Cr(VI)-reduction assays. The fabrication steps for the living MF-derived biocomposites are illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The Cr(VI) reduction induced by individual cells (nanoscale), biofilms (microscale) and living biocomposites (macroscale) was in turn evaluated as a progressive sequence. To confirm if hybrid concretion of individual cells was beneficial to Cr(VI) reduction, a Cr(VI) reduction experiment was conducted by incubating with different active configured cells under a given illuminated condition. Reaction systems were amended with (i) wild strain + dark, (ii) wild strain + illumination, (iii) biohybrids + dark, and (iv) biohybrids + illumination. All biosystems were reacted in 40.0 mL anaerobic BTP medium, which corresponded to a given biomass (OD600 ≈ 0.1), 50 mg/L Cr(VI) and 20 mM lactate. The composition of the anaerobic BTP medium is shown in Table S1. All experiments were performed in triplicate and reacted under anaerobic conditions in 50 mL serum bottles at 30 °C. Prior to incubation, anaerobic BTP mediums were deoxygenated by N2 bubbling for 1 h and then autoclaved for 20 min at 120 °C. To create a strictly anaerobic system, all incubation procedures were performed in an anaerobic glovebox, and serum bottles filled with anaerobic BTP medium were firstly bubbled with N2 for 1 h and then tightly sealed with a rubber septum and aluminum seals. To create a platform for visible-light photocatalysis, the visible light source was supplied with white LED irradiation at an energy level of ∼50 μmol/m2·s.

Another batch of assays was prepared to explore the biocompatibilities of different biofilms (formed by wild strain/biohybrids with/without PPy-immobilization) in MF-derived composites. Therein, the composite with 0.30 mL PPy-immobilization was selected as the representative MF/PPy composite. Likewise, Cr(VI) reduction experiments were reacted in a 50 mL serum bottle that included one piece of living MF-derived biocomposite, 50 mg/L Cr(VI) and 20 mM lactate in a 40 mL anaerobic BTP medium. The removal of Cr(VI) in dark and illuminated conditions was evaluated in the presence of living or sterilized MF-derived composites. The maximum Cr(VI) reduction efficiency and corresponding reaction time for each system were recorded to determine the best combined metrics for further optimization.

Based on the aforementioned results, living MF/PPy biocomposites were decorated with gradient amounts of PY monomer (0.15, 0.30, 0.45 and 0.60 mL) to further evaluate their enhanced efficacies for Cr(VI) reduction and potential for use as a recyclable living biocatalyst. Cr(VI) reduction cycles (4 successive runs) were performed in the presence of different living MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites under specific reaction conditions. Reaction parameters (including irradiation and biofilms) were ascertained from the combined results of the above-mentioned experiments. When residual Cr(VI) concentrations fell below 0.01 mg/L, the reactions were stopped and the biocomposites were recovered for a subsequent reuse cycle. The operating efficiency of the recycled living biocatalysts was determined from their performance for (i) Cr(VI) reduction and Cr(T) (total chromium) adsorption, (ii) reaction time required for completed Cr(VI) reduction, and (iii) stability of immobilized biofilms during each reaction cycle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr species and concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: Concentrations of Cr(T) (total chromium) and Cr(VI) were determined using an ICP-MS (NexION, 2000; PerkinElmer, USA) and the 1, 5-diphenylcarbazide (DPC) spectrophotometric method, respectively (J.-C. Han et al., 2017). Prior to quantification, 1.5 mL of supernatant were extracted from serum bottles using a sterilized syringe and then filtered through a 0.22-μm filter. The precipitate deposited on the surface of the biocomposite skeleton was collected for speciation of Cr(VI) reduction products using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy microprobe (Quantum-2000, USA) (M. Mullet et al., 2007).

The biomass of bacterial fluid was estimated by measuring OD600 values using a UV/VIS spectrophotometer (Cary 60, Agilent, USA). Concurrently, the dry cell weight of biomass was measured to evaluate its correlation to OD600 values (Fig. S7). Therein, 1.0 mL of bacterial fluid was extracted from the inoculums and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 2 min to harvest the cell cluster. Cells were then washed with Milli-Q water thrice, and finally dried at 60 °C to constant weight to obtain the dry cell weight. Additionally, the bicinchoninic acid (BCA) assay was employed to evaluate the biomass incorporated into the biofilms following the protocols for the BCA Protein Quantification Kit (Yeasen, China) (J. Cortés-Ríos et al., 2020).

Dissolved organic carbon (DOC) extracted from the supernatant was measured using a TOC analyzer (Shimadzu, TOC-L CPH, Japan) to estimate the degradation of organic carbon associated with the supply of bio-electrons and photoelectrons. Prior to determination of TOC, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter (Millipore, USA).

Characterization techniques were employed to analyze the physical and chemical properties of MF-derived composites. The morphologies of bacterial cells, biofilms and composited materials were characterized by SEM (Sigma 300, Zeiss, Germany) and TEM (TALOS F200, FEI, USA). Changes in functional group content of the MF-derived composite skeletons were detected using Attenuated Total Reflection-Fourier Transform Infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet 330, Thermo Fischer, USA). Specific surface area, pore volume and electrical conductivity of modified composites were determined using a BET analyzer (ASAP 2020, Micromeritics, USA) and an electrical conductivity meter (Novocontrol Concept 90, Germany).

Cr(VI) removal by treatment with different hybrid cells was described using a pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model. Further, Cr(VI) reduction efficiency (corresponding to average Cr(VI) removal degree by individual cells and reaction time), as well as the overall TOC consumption and related TOC consumption per cell, were calculated according to Equations S1–S3 in supporting information. A more detailed discussion for estimation of Cr(VI) removal and TOC consumption is reported in Method S3 of SM. Data were analyzed and displayed using Origin 9.0, Microsoft Excel 2016 and SPSS 20.0 software. Each data point represents the average value of at least three measurements. Standard deviations for triplicate analyses were reported as error bars in the corresponding graphs. Treatment differences were examined by analysis of variance using SPSS with a 95% confidence level.

The ﬂuids of wild strain and biohybrids were characterized by image analyses under purple light irradiation and TEM observation (Fig. 2 ). Contrasting with the purple ﬂuids of the wild strain, the color of biohybrid ﬂuids is similar to that of the blue-emission NCDs under purple light irradiation. A unique shell-like structure formed by PDA is created in the biohybrids, as compared to the bare and smooth outer-membranes in the wild strain. Higher magnification TEM images show a large number of tiny NCD nanospheres densely inlaid into the PDA layer. Moreover, measurement of micro-dimensions relative to a randomly discrete distributed spot indicates a precise match of particle size with the NCD's size, confirming an outward configuration of NCDs in the PDA layers. These results substantiate that we successfully created living photosensitive hybrid cells (denoted as biohybrids) that are coated with dual shells of NCDs and PDA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced microbial-photoreduction of Cr(VI) by biohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to immobilization of bulk biofilms, a preliminary investigation was performed to assess hybrid cell survivability and biocatalytic efficacy in order to create a flexible conformational structure for individual cells at the nanoscale. A poor Cr(VI) reduction efficiency (only 5%) was obtained from the wild strain in the dark (Fig. 3 a). In contrast, irradiated wild strains exhibited a stronger Cr(VI) reduction efficiency (nearly 78%) than that of wild strain in the dark, indicating that photocatalytic reduction contributes considerably to Cr(VI) reduction in this system (Y. Zhang et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the biohybrids possessed a much stronger biocatalytic capability for Cr(VI) reduction than the wild strain regardless of dark or illuminated conditions. Under illuminated conditions, complete Cr(VI) removal (100%) is achieved after reacting for 120 h with a limited biomass (initial OD600 ≈ 0.14) of biohybrids, which is up to 21.8, 2.3 and 5.0 times of that of the treatments with wild strains + dark, wild strains + illumination, and biohybrids + dark, respectively. The pronounced catalytic effect is also illustrated by the highest rate constant (0.0197 h−1) for a pseudo-first-order reaction (Fig. 3b). This enhancement resulted from irradiated biohybrids using visible light to harvest more photoelectrons from irradiated semiconducting NCDs while quenching photoholes in the PDA layer without adding any sacrifice reagents (K. K. Sakimoto et al., 2016), thereby ensuring more efficient Cr(VI) reduction than achieved by the wild strain. Moreover, irradiated wild strains contributed an obviously higher Cr(VI) reduction than that of dark (Fig. 3a). This was mainly attributed to more electrons supplied from the photodegradation of BTP contained in the anaerobic medium and thereby injected into the Cr(VI) reduction process (H. D. Duan et al., 2021). Even in dark conditions, the biohybrids enabled a stronger Cr(VI) bioreduction than the wild strain (increased by 3.5 times). The enhancement in the dark was likely due to the ability of NCDs to directly induced Cr(VI) reduction and accepted bio-electrons supplied from stimulated microbial metabolism processes in the biohybrids (as inferred from increased TOC consumption; Fig. 3e) (D. Guo et al., 2019; S. Liu et al., 2020; C. Yang et al., 2020).

Since Cr(VI) bioreduction is a dynamic process involving transformation of Cr(VI) to Cr(III), it also influences bacterial growth (Fig. 3c) as Cr(VI) bioreduction contributed by different individual cells is believed to exclude the combined effects by greater biomass. According to the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency calculated on a per unit biomass basis, the maximum efficiency of illuminated biohybrids is nearly 6.0 times of that of the wild strain in the dark (Fig. 3d). The biohybrids display a higher degree of overall TOC bioavailability than the wild strain in the dark, whereas they show a lower TOC bioavailability under illuminated conditions (Fig. 3e). Based on the relative TOC consumption calculated on a per cell basis (Fig. 3f), biohybrids incubated in illuminated conditions show the lowest TOC consumption (49% and 27% that of the wild strain incubated in dark and illuminated conditions, respectively).

The results indicating improved Cr(VI) removal and mitigated TOC consumption by irradiated biohybrids than wild strain demonstrate that illumination and assembly of NCDs were more effective for Cr(VI) reduction. Enhanced Cr(VI) reduction by irradiated biohybrids may be attributed to visible-light irradiation creating a beneficial environment for enlarging the electron flux through integrating the harvest of photoelectrons from irradiated NCDs together with the photodegradation of BTP. Notably, irradiated biohybrids can preferentially utilize visible-light energy (e.g., sunlight) to harvest photoelectrons, thereby reducing their consumption of organic carbon substrates. Thus, the successful fabrication of biohybrids cells was demonstrated to produce effective microbial-photoreduction on a nanoscale. Overall, the biohybrids performed well in Cr(VI) reduction and their properties meet the requirements for visible light-derived photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and physicochemical characterization of MF-derived biocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 reveals the color and size of pristine MF and MF/PPy composites. Although the color of pristine MF changed (from white to black) after decorating with PPy, the size was the same as the pristine form (Fig. 4a-b). A mechanical strength test performed by manually pinching indicates a favorable compressibility of MF/PPy composites and ensures that the fabricated composites were suitable for practical application (Fig. 4c). SEM images (Fig. 4d) show that numerous skeletons are interconnected into a three-dimensional (3D) network with an aperture size ranging from 30 to 150 μm in pristine MF. This morphology demonstrates that MF offers a good scaffold for PPy-polymerization and bacterial immobilization. With an increased amount of PPy used for polymerization, more PPy NPs are distributed on the surface of the MF skeletons.

SEM-EDS images (Fig. 4e, f and 4g) of slices cut from the MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposite provide direct evidence for Cr sorption on the MF skeleton surface. A greater distribution of Fe and N was revealed by EDS characterization (quantitative results in Fig. 4h and Fig. S8) for the composites of MF/PPy and pristine MF. This indicates a substantial polymerization effect of PPy, which withstands at least 4 reduction-use cycles because of the greater amounts of Fe and N (which are sourced from FeCl3 and PY monomer) participating in PPy-polymerization. Compared to the thin biofilms formed by the wild strain, thick biohybrids-derived biofilms are clustered around dense biohybrids and completely covered by PPy spherical particles. The biofilm layers increased layer-by-layer in the PPy-junction (revealed by SEM images of biofilms cut from the slice, Fig. 4i and j). The SEM image (Fig. 4j) of aggregated biofilms at PPy-junction sites indicates reliable bacterial attachment in the overlapping biofilm formed in the PPy immobilization architecture. The effect of PPy-modification was further verified in subsequent experiments (sections 3.3 and 3.5).

FTIR spectra (Fig. 5 a) reveal a shift in the composition of functional groups resulting from PPy-modification. Compared to pristine MF, significant stretching vibration bands at 1558, 1284, 1159 and 770 cm−1 appeared in the FTIR spectra of MF/PPy composites. The bands at 1558 and 1284 cm−1 are assigned to CC and C–C bending vibrations and C–N stretching in PY rings (S. Shrikrushna et al., 2015), whereas the bands at 1159 and 770 cm−1 are assigned to C–N and C–H stretching in PY rings (G. Świderski et al., 2011; Q. Zhen et al., 2020). For PPy/MF composites decorated with 0.45 and 0.60 mL of PY monomers, stretching vibrations are prominently displayed for the characteristic peaks corresponding to C–H and N–H groups (961 and 1558 cm−1). We attribute these characteristics to hydrogen atoms in melamine being replaced by nitrogen atoms from the PY monomers (Y. Erdogdu et al., 2015; S. Shrikrushna et al., 2015). The stretching vibrations for the characteristic functional groups of PPy displayed in MF/PPy composites confirm a reliable formation of PPy in the MF skeleton.

BET analysis (Fig. 5b) indicates a higher specific surface area and pore volume with increasing PPy amounts in PPy-modified composites. Since PPy-polymerization is an intensive exothermic process (Y. C. Wong et al., 2023), the reaction resulting from a larger amount of PY monomer will create additional gases at high temperature and pressure that create greater structural porosity. Thus, the pore volume of modified MF was correspondingly increased due to a larger production of narrow micropores when adding higher amounts of PY monomer for PPy-polymerization. Consistent with BET analysis, we found that PPy-coating lead to a pronounced enhancement (nearly 8–29 times) in the electrical conductivity of pristine MF (Fig. 5c). This implies that a unique abiotic/biotic coupling junction (e.g., MF-PPy, PPy-biofilms and biofilm/PPy-Cr(VI)), as well as their overlapping heterojunction structures, create an ordered-layer, multidirectional and highly conductive architecture, which contribute to more efficient EET processes.

Precipitates from the bottom of serum bottles and shaved from the MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposite were collected for XPS analysis to characterize Cr speciation. Based on XPS analysis (Fig. 5d), Cr 2p peaks were grouped into four peaks. The peaks at 577.4 and 584.7 eV were assigned to Cr(III) 2p3/2 and Cr(III) 2p1/2 (Y. Li et al., 2019), while the peaks at 580.1 and 586.9 eV represented Cr(VI) 2p3/2 and Cr(VI) 2p1/2 (L. Xiang et al., 2021). The integrated areas of fitted peaks for Cr speciation confirmed that the initially present Cr(VI) was substantially (>87.4%) transformed into Cr(III) precipitates. These findings demonstrate that PPy-modifications provide more flexibility and applicability to the function of living biocomposites, either partially or in their entirety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of Cr(VI) in the presence of living MF-derived biocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Wild strain cells or biohybrid cells with the same initial biomass were incubated and immobilized to form different MF/biofilm biocomposites with or without PPy-decoration (Fig. 6 ). Active biofilms formed in MF exhibit a better efficacy (up to 13–100%) for Cr(VI) reduction than inactive biofilms (only 3–67%) under the same illuminated conditions. Notably, a more pronounced Cr(VI) reduction performance is obtained in the biofilm treatment immobilized onto MF with PPy-modification versus PPy-free decoration. In contrast, the active biocomposite of MF/PPy/WS achieves the desirable effect of complete Cr(VI) removal (100%) even in dark conditions, and the corresponding effect is up to 7.5 times that of the PPy-free biocomposite after 120 h (Fig. 6b). Similarly, PPy-modification provides a favorable effect toward the stability of biofilms produced with biohybrids cells. Active MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites enable a stronger Cr(VI) reduction capacity than biocomposites immobilized with wild strain cells. Active biocomposites had reaction times at least 24 h faster under illuminated conditions (Fig. 6a vs Fig. 6c) and Cr(VI) reduction efficiency was increased by 19% under dark conditions after 96 h (Fig. 6b vs Fig. 6d).

A summary of treatment effects for Cr(VI) reduction is visualized in Fig. 7 based on optimum efficiency and reaction times under corresponding irradiated conditions. Herein, a suitable design for engineering high-efficiency biocatalysts for Cr(VI) bioremediation involves employing modular configurations for MF through immobilizing biohybrids biofilms by in-situ PPy-junctions along with irradiated conditions. Therein, the amount of PPy used for the immobilization of biofilms serves as a critical factor in determining the ultimate Cr(VI) reduction efficiency. The improved efficacy owing to PPy-modifications is attributed to larger biofilms formed at the surface of the MF skeleton, as well as enhanced charge transport capability at PPy-junction sites resulting from the favorable hydrophilicity and conductivity associated with PPy-junction sites (W. Qian et al., 2017; R.-B. Song et al., 2017). Therefore, these two parameters were further investigated in follow-up experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recyclability of PPy-optimized biocatalyst used for microbial-photoreduction of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the impacts of PPy on the stability of immobilized biofilms and capabilities for reuse in subsequent Cr(VI) reduction cycles, we fabricated different MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites and evaluated their stabilities and recyclability. The overall biocatalytic efficacies of fabricated biocomposites are significantly increased with increasing amounts of immobilized PPy onto MFs (Fig. 8 ). For instance, biocomposites modified with lower amounts of PPy (0.15 and 0.30 mL PY monomer) exhibit a comparably poorer capacity for Cr(VI) reduction as demonstrated by longer reaction times (72–120 h and 48–72 h) for complete Cr(VI) reductions during all four testing cycles (Fig. 8a–b). Meanwhile, their biocatalytic abilities dramatically weakened after 3 cycles and their related reaction times were extended by at least 48 and 24 h in the fourth cycle.

Decorated biocomposites using greater PY monomer amounts (0.45 and 0.60 mL) for junction formation achieve a considerably stronger performance (Fig. 8c–d). In particular, the biocomposites modified with 0.60 mL of PY monomer display the highest efficiency and shortest reaction times (only 24 h) for complete Cr(VI) reduction during the first reduction cycle; efficiency remained high, but reaction time was extended to 60 h by the fourth reaction cycle (Fig. 8d and Table S2). Since MF-derived composites contain a porous structure and PPy-coated layers are positively charged, the negatively charged Cr(VI) ions are readily adsorbed to the MF skeleton through electrostatic attraction (L. Zhang et al., 2020). This Cr adsorption effect leads to an attenuated biocatalytic effect in subsequent reduction cycles due to a lower effective contact area among PPy-junction sites resulting from deposition of Cr(III)-bearing precipitates. This mechanism is supported by the Cr(T) adsorption dynamics across reduction cycles and the EDS distribution (Fig. 4h). Namely, there is a remarkable attenuation of Cr(T) adsorption performance in subsequent reduction cycles in the presence of biocomposites as Cr(III)-bearing precipitates accumulate (Fig. S9). Moreover, no black precipitates derived from PPy were leached before or after a reaction cycle (Fig. S10) indicating a stable PPy-junction in the biocomposites that inhibited leaching throughout progressive reaction cycles.

Biofilm biomass and stability of MFs during each reduction cycle were further investigated (Fig. 9 ). Herein, the BCA test quantifies immobilized biofilms on the MF, whereas OD600 values indicate the planktonic biomass dissociated from biofilms to the liquid phase. Planktonic biomass in the liquid phase is relatively small as inferred by the low OD600 values (<0.025). In contrast, biofilm mass on all biocomposites was pronounced and maintained levels close to their initial values during each subsequent reduction cycle. This suggests that PPy-junctions afford a favorable refuge for biofilms to sustain their stabilities. There are two main factors likely to influence bacterial attachment onto the PPy layer. First, the unique surface properties of the biotic and abiotic components ensure a close interaction of bacterial cells onto the PPy layer through electrostatic attraction owing to oppositely charged biohybrids (negative) and PPy (positive) surfaces (Table S3) (Z. Chen et al., 2022; C. Li and S. Cheng et al., 2021). Second, PPy-coating provides superior hydrophilicity and biocompatibility for bacterial attachments and thus makes for a good interface to promote bacterial colonization and attachment, thereby resulting in increased thickness and roughness of the biofilm communities (M. Golabi et al., 2016; B. Jiang et al., 2020). These findings provide compelling evidence for the growth and long-term viability of the immobilized biofilms on the PPy layer.

Although PPy has been reported to reduce Cr(VI) through electron-rich PPy moieties (like amine groups) (L. Xiang et al., 2021), the Cr(VI) reduction mediated by PPy alone was very limited in our study. PPy-induced Cr(VI) reduction was only 2% as estimated from the difference for Cr(VI) removal between inactive MF/biohybrids and inactive MF/PPy/biohybrids in the dark (Fig. 6d). This infers that Cr(VI) removal was mainly ascribed to the synergistic effects of the illuminated living MF/PPy/biohybrids biocomposites, in which all components (biofilms, electrically conductive interface and electron flux) of electron transport were strongly integrated. For example, the living biocomposites with 0.60 mL PY for immobilization achieved the highest catalytic effect for Cr(VI) reduction. The enhancement by 0.60 mL of PY was due to its effect on creating the thickest biofilms (Fig. 9a) that were immobilized on the surface of MF interfaces. This material had the largest specific surface area/pore volume (Fig. 5b) that provided bacterial attachment sites and the highest conductivity (Fig. 5c). Since PPy is biocompatible, highly conductive and of opposite charge (positive) with respect to the bacterial outer-membrane surface (negative) (Z. Chen et al., 2022), the optimal integration by the 0.6 mL PY junction promoted formation of the largest biofilms and created the most scalable, flexible and efficient avenue to maximize EET efficiency. Overall, the resulting material creates a reliable hybrid framework integrating a flexible conformational structure with sufficient energetic catalysis efficiency to warrant practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of driving forces on Cr(VI) reduction at different scales
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 10 , wild strain cells possessing a low Cr(VI) reduction capability are firstly implanted with NCDs and then outwardly coated with PDA and NCDs to create a unique core/shell structure. Our previous investigation demonstrated that living biohybrids could not only integrate light energy from the extracellular environment but also synergize the inherent functions of natural cells, such as increased production of c-type cytochrome and flavin as well as an enlarged NAD (H/+) pool on a cellular level. With the additional reducing power supplied from NCDs, Cr(VI) reduction efficiency by the living photosensitive biohybrids was prominently increased and outperformed the wild strain cells by at least 6-fold (Fig. 3d). Thus, an appropriate modification targeting individual cells at the nanoscale is identified as an effective avenue to enhance natural cell functionalities.

To achieve a holistic high performance of charge transfer coordinated by individual cells, an augmented electron flux was extended to biofilm modification on a microscale. Herein, we find PPy-modification provides a compatible cell–material interaction and also affords a suitable structure over a range of biologically relevant spatial scales. The increased surface wettability induced by PPy-coating is beneficial to create a humid environment for bacterial attachment, thereby promoting augmented biofilm formation. Furthermore, benefits from PPy-induced enhanced electrical conductivity and improved direct contact-based extracellular electron transfer in bacterial cells results in a bulk electron cascade across cell-PPy hybrid bio-interfaces (J. Li et al., 2022; R.-B. Song et al., 2017). Thus, PPy-modifications rebalance the allocation of cellular resources for bacterial growth to ensure subsequent EET enhancement as well as increasing the habitat quality for the living biomaterials.

Beyond modification of microbial components across the nanoscale to microscale continuum, the enhancement of living biomaterials emphasizes the transition from an initial static to eventual dynamic state by incorporating dynamic features into the biomaterials (T. Biswal et al., 2020). Considering the porous structure of MF and positive charge of the PPy polymer layer, a facile strategy was employed via PPy-modification, which enables an in-situ planting of biofilms on the MF skeletons and effective adsorption of negatively charged Cr(VI) ions through electrostatic attraction (L. Zhang et al., 2020; Y. Y. Zhao et al., 2022). Correspondingly these biocomposites provide a hierarchically-ordered architecture at the macroscale that offers a reliable mechanical support for biofilm attachment while also accelerating Cr(VI) reduction at larger spatiotemporal scales (Sonu et al., 2019, 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design function of each component comprising the recyclable living biocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to Cr(VI) reduction capacity and reaction times examined in recent investigations (Table 1 ), the fabricated MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites developed in this study represent an advanced biocatalyst with a superior performance for Cr(VI) remediation. Overall, the resulting material creates a reliable hybrid framework integrating a flexible conformational structure with sufficient energetic catalysis efficiency to warrant practical field application. With regard to advancing environmental applications, the favorable characteristics of high efficiency reduction, energy savings and recyclability are novel features of the advanced biomaterial (X. Li et al., 2020; P. Q. Nguyen et al., 2018; T.-C. Tang et al., 2021). Owing to the collaborative effects between the essential components of biofilms, PPy and MF, the functionality and utility of living biocomposites provide management improvements including efficient interactive collaborations, rapid response and orderly coordination of component parts along with strong mechanical strength (C. Li and S. Cheng et al., 2021; D.-F. Liu and W.-W. Li et al., 2020). Therefore, the merits of living biocomposites increase environmental adaptability and their holistic mechanical/catalytic performance permits an excellent recyclable material for Cr(VI) remediation.

In this work, we accomplished the design of a living biocomposite by building the material from simple to complex, from space to an intricate shape, and from the local to the wholly-integrated structure to realize an optimized catalytic effect for Cr(VI) reduction. We firstly synthesized NCDs providing visible-light catalyzed and Cr(VI) reductive properties using waste straw. Thereafter, a series of processing techniques was conducted to construct a bulk living biomaterial, which was then evaluated for its ability to remediate Cr(VI) in wastewater. The processing techniques enable the manufacturing of a stable and high-performance living biomaterial with outstanding biochemical and biomechanical features that vary across several size scales (nano, micro and macro), as well as corresponding levels of complexity.

The living biocomposites fabricated in this work could be embedded in packed bed bioreactors and variations of this approach are expected to broaden the toolbox of techniques available for Cr(VI) remediation of wastewater and soils at larger scales. In addition, a Cr resource could be recovered in the form of Cr2O3 through simple calcination of MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites after reaction. Calcination avoids utilization of environmentally-harmful desorption liquids, while greatly simplifying the metal recovery process. Hence, this novel approach involving “recycled Cr(VI) reduction-calcination-Cr recovery” achieves a win-win outcome for remediation of Cr pollution from wastewaters. Overall, the living MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposite represents an efficient new environmental-friendly remediation technology with “one waste treating another waste”, given that agricultural waste straw was used to manufacture the living biomaterials for use in treating Cr(VI)-polluted wastewaters in a highly efficient, recyclable and energy-saving manner (Sonu et al., 2023). Further, the application prospects are also expected to achieve a practical recovery of the Cr resource from wastewaters and other Cr contaminated environments.

A highly efficient and recyclable living biocatalyst comprised of photosensitive biohybrids, PPy and MF was engineered through modular configurations for use in microbial-photoreductive Cr(VI) remediation. The functionality of the living biocatalyst was augmented following a progressive approach: individual cells → biofilms → bulk biocomposite. Correspondingly, the catalytic effects of the living biocatalyst were enhanced due to (i) an augmented foreign supply of photoelectrons and NCD-derived reducing powers in the biohybrids (nanoscale), (ii) a boosted biohybrids reduction capacity ensuing from improved electrical conductivity within PPy-coated biofilms (microscale), and (iii) shortened reduction reaction times resulting from a larger reactive surface area in the three-dimensional porous structure of MF-derived composites (macroscale). The fine-scaled architecture of living biocomposites harnesses a reliable, efficiency-configuration framework to achieve a high Cr(VI) reduction capacity, as well as permitting several recycled uses. The methodology reported in this study highlights the advantages of exploiting advanced living biomaterials and living MF/PPy/biohybrid biocomposites for efficient Cr(VI) remediation while achieving energy savings, recyclability and high efficiency attributes.
